# Workforce & Training

Workforce and training issues are critical to organizational success and economic prosperity. They encompass talent acquisition, employee retention, skills development, diversity and inclusion, work-life balance and labor relations. Addressing these issues effectively can boost productivity, innovation and competitiveness while enhancing employee satisfaction and well-being. With advancing technologies, a limited workforce, and a vast number of engineers retiring, attention to your workforce and operator training is more crucial than ever.

--- SOURCE 3: The enabling technologies for digitalization in the chemical process ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231

SUMMARY:
The enabling technologies for digitalization in the chemical process industry - ScienceDirect The enabling technologies for digitalization in the chemical process industry In this paper, we provide an overview of the technologies that enable digitalization in the chemical process industry and describe their applications to solve problems in industrial settings. Furthermore, we identify the problem domains that characterize the chemical process industry and connect them to development aspects in the industry that lend themselves to digital solutions. Specifically, our aim is to provide an introductory overview of the enabling technologies for digitalization to practitioners in the chemical process industry with no prior digitalization background. 2025, Digital Chemical Engineering  Show abstract Digital Twins (DTs) are transforming industrial processes by providing virtual models that mirror physical systems, enabling real-time monitoring and optimization.

Original Article

The enabling technologies for digitalization in the chemical process industry
===============================================================================================

Highlights
----------

*   •Identified and categorized the enabling technologies for digitalization. 
*   •Identified the problem domains and development aspects that characterize the chemical process industry. 
*   •Selected the technologies most essential to bridging the gap between problem and solution. 
*   •Provided case studies to cast a spotlight on the use of state-of-the-art technologies. 

Abstract
--------

In this paper, we provide an overview of the technologies that enable digitalization in the chemical [process industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/process-industry "Learn more about process industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and describe their applications to solve problems in industrial settings. This is done through the identification and categorization of these technologies, thereby providing structure to an otherwise loosely connected basket of technologies and casting a spotlight on state-of-the-art technologies that offer great potential but are still underutilized in industrial applications. Furthermore, we identify the problem domains that characterize the chemical process [industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and connect them to development aspects in the industry that lend themselves to digital solutions. For each of these connections, we select the technologies most essential to bridging the gap between problem and solution. This allows practitioners to better understand the [relevancy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/relevancy "Learn more about relevancy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of digitalization to their problems and provides a starting point for further investigation of potential solutions. The connections are substantiated by reference to successful industrial applications, highlighting previous works that have been published in the field. They are further verified by industry experts through brainstorm sessions, interviews, and a workshop.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277250812400022X)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000243)

Keywords
--------

Digitalization

Chemical process industry

Enabling technology

1. Introduction
---------------

It has been over a decade since a team of German scientists published a document outlining a strategic plan for the German industrial sector. The document ([Kagermann et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b171)), translated into English as _Industrie 4.0: With the_[Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_Towards the Fourth Industrial Revolution_, outlined a vision of a future [industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in which the real and virtual worlds are intertwined through widespread digitalization ([Kagermann and Wahlster, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b172)). According to the authors, this digitalization transformation would bring about the fourth industrial revolution and mark a radical shift in manufacturing processes; a shift akin to those of the three industrial revolutions that came before it. The idea garnered much attention at the Hanover Fair of Industrial Technologies later that year and its fulfilment has been a growing priority for enterprises ever since. According to a 2019 report by McKinsey ([Garms et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b122)), 68 percent of companies surveyed consider Industry 4.0 a top strategic priority, whereas only 10 percent deem it a low priority. At the level of policymaking, the term too has infiltrated the mainstream consciousness. Angela Merkel was quick to pick up on the term and spread it outside of the German-speaking world in her 2011 address to a crowd of industry leaders ([Kagermann and Wahlster, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b172)). The founder and chairman of the World Economic Forum, Klaus Schwab, penned an influential article for _Foreign Affairs_ ([Schwab, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b282)) stressing the impact that the fourth industrial revolution will have on businesses, governments, and people. The ideas in this article were incorporated into the famed Great Reset Initiative as a response to the COVID-19 pandemic ([Schwab and Malleret, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b283)). With this in mind, there is no doubt that the fourth industrial revolution and digitalization are a key concern in the world today. But carrying out these industrial changes is harder than merely writing about them and behind the slogans and buzzwords there must be concrete solutions and roadmaps to implement.

To this end, substantial progress has been made over the past decade in implementing the changes necessary for the digitalization transformation to take place. In a 2020 report on the degree of adoption of Industry 4.0 principles by enterprises, 80 percent of the companies surveyed have taken the first steps towards digitalization ([Schuh et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b281)). This is especially true for large enterprises, nearly all of which have begun their digitalization transformation. Moreover, companies such as Nokia ([Nokia, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b232)), Siemens ([Helmrich, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b149)), and Intel ([Pulsipher, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b254)) have made Industry 4.0 solutions a key part of their manufacturing processes. In the industrial sector, digitalization is increasingly seen as a competitive advantage. Companies that do not undergo such a transformation are expected to endanger their viability against competitors that do ([Winter et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b347)). At the level of government and policymaking, there too has been a concentrated focus on pushing industrial digitalization forward as a means of staying competitive in the global marketplace. In the past decade, countries such as [Germany](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/germany "Learn more about Germany from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([DIN e. V. and Deutsche Kommission Elektrotechnik, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b89)), France ([European Comission, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b99)), and the United Kingdom ([Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b85)) have put forward plans and standards to help companies implement digital solutions and modernize their production pipelines. These plans are backed by funding programs which provide industry and academia with the monetary means for their realization.

One sector which has taken a relatively conservative approach to digitalization is the chemical [process industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/process-industry "Learn more about process industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). According to a 2023 report by McKinsey, digital technologies are still underutilized by many companies in the industry ([Falkenroth-Naidu et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b104)). A similar study conducted by PTC found that only 35 percent of chemical companies were in the rollout phase of their digitalization journey compared to an industry average of 41 percent ([Immerman, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b162)). This is the second lowest rate of the industries examined. An older report ranked the challenges preventing further digitalization in the chemical process industry and found that the top three factors are high investment costs, lack of digital culture and training, and a lack of vision for digitalization ([Westerman et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b344)). It is the second of these factors that is addressed in our work. Specifically, our aim is to provide an introductory overview of the enabling technologies for digitalization to practitioners in the chemical process industry with no prior digitalization background. The work takes a structured and holistic view of how digital technologies relate to one another and solve the challenges that are faced by the field broadly. It is thus intended to be viewed as a starting point from which more concrete solutions can be explored. Furthermore, we believe that such a work can serve as an anchor for the existing disparate approaches that fall under the umbrella of digital [chemical engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/chemical-engineering "Learn more about chemical engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

We further explore the motivations for this work in Section [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#sec2). Section [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#sec3) provides a brief overview of the industry’s landscape and current challenges as well as an introduction to the enabling technologies that can be leveraged to solve them. The connections between industry problems and aspects of digital development are made in Section [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#sec4). These connections are substantiated through concrete examples of real-world industrial applications of digital technologies in Section [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#sec5) and evaluated in Section [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#sec6). Section [7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#sec7) concludes the paper.

2. Research outline and approach
--------------------------------

Before proceeding with the remainder of the paper, we outline our research by describing the goals it aims to accomplish and the process by which it does so. The work is performed as part of Brightsite,[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#fn1) an initiative formed by university, research organization, and [industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") partners with the goal of transforming the [chemical industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/chemical-industry "Learn more about chemical industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") towards a more sustainable future through the development and application of technologies that advance its [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), safety, and [human capital](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/human-capital "Learn more about human capital from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") objectives ([Stokking and Slaghek, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b302)). One of the program lines at Brightsite is the deployment of digital technologies within the chemical industrial park Chemelot[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#fn2) in Geleen, The Netherlands. In order to deploy these digital technologies, it is first necessary to understand them and how they may be applied to solve concrete problems. This provides the motivation for our work. More concretely, we break down the aims of this paper into the following:

*   •Identify and categorize the enabling technologies for digitalization, thereby providing structure to an otherwise loosely connected basket of technologies. 
*   •Identify the problem domains that characterize the chemical [process industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/process-industry "Learn more about process industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and connect them to development aspects in the industry that lend themselves to digital solutions. 
*   •For each of these connections, select the technologies most essential to bridging the gap between problem and solution. 
*   •Provide [case studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case studies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to cast a spotlight on the use of state-of-the-art technologies that offer great potential but are still underutilized in an industrial context. 

These aims differ from those of existing works such as those by [Oztemel and Gursev (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b239) that focuses on digitalization in industry broadly or [Al-Rbeawi (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b12) that focuses on digitalization more narrowly, specifically in the [oil and gas industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/petroleum-industry "Learn more about oil and gas industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Furthermore, they are broader in scope than work that focuses on reviewing the use of a specific technology in the industry such as artificial intelligence [He et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b148) and [Alimi and Meijboom (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b15). The aims are accomplished by synthesizing existing scholarly work in the fields of digitalization and [chemical engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/chemical-engineering "Learn more about chemical engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with real-world experiences from chemical plants and the latest developments in their commercial applications. In other words, we integrate both academic and industry perspectives in our work. More concretely, we leverage academic literature to identify and elucidate enabling technologies for digitalization. Case studies, whether published as scholarly work, grey literature, or press releases, are used to demonstrate and exemplify the connections between the theoretical and practical domains. Opinions of domain experts in the Dutch chemicals industry, primarily those with connections to Chemelot, are used to gain insight into industry perspectives. Specifically, we obtained expert information via the following:

*   •Brainstorming sessions with Brightsite digitalization team. 
*   •Semi-structured interviews with practitioners in the chemical process industry. 
*   •Workshop with digitalization experts connected to the Chemelot industrial park. 

The brainstorming sessions provided the impetus for our work by identifying the absence of a technology overview in the academic literature and its necessity for further efforts at reaching the industry’s digitalization objectives. In addition to this, the problem domains and development aspects we use as the framework for organizing the digital transformation were conceived in this setting. These problem domains and development aspects were verified by domain experts in the form of semi-structured interviews. These interviews provided us a starting point for drawing connections between problems in the industry and the enabling technologies that can be used to solve them. These technologies were presented to digitalization allies through a workshop whose goal was to gain insight into the usefulness, ease of implementation, and intention to use of each technology, drawing inspiration from the [Technology Acceptance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/technology-acceptance "Learn more about Technology Acceptance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Model ([Davis, 1989](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b81)). Details of this model and how it was leveraged in our evaluation are described in Section [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#sec6). The specifics of all expert interactions are provided in [Appendix](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#appendix).

The interviews and workshop not only allowed us to verify our work but also provides motivation for it. When speaking of his own company – an industrial utility provider for chemical plants – one interviewee is quoted as saying: “What would be helpful is knowing what these digital technologies can do and easily access successful implementation examples at similar companies to mine…We are not interested in the technologies that are for car manufacturers or ASML, we want to know if there is something which we also can apply and that we do not have to find everything out ourselves”. This sentiment was echoed by a program manager at Brightsite: “You don’t know what you don’t know and so you need information as to what is possible and how it can support you and help you. At the same time, you have to have some kind of question or opportunity or problem that makes it worthwhile to dig deeper”. In identifying, describing, and outlining the use of the enabling technologies for digitalization, we aim to educate and inspire industry professionals to investigate and undertake digitalization initiatives in their practice.

3. Background
-------------

In this section, we first discuss the problems facing the chemical process industry as discovered in our interviews and by reviewing the existing literature. For each of these problems, we highlight how digitalization provides opportunities for their overcoming. After this, we provide an overview of the enabling technologies for digitalization, thus providing background knowledge before relating them to specific problem domains, development aspects, and applications later in our work.

### 3.1. Problems and opportunities facing the chemical process industry

The most common industry challenge brought up during our interviews was that of a skills shortage in the [labour market](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/labor-market "Learn more about labour market from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Indeed, this is a problem that is neither recent ([Besson and Haddadj, 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b40)) nor unique to the chemical process industry ([European Labour Authority, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b101)). The problem is particularly pronounced in Western nations where a lack of young talent is unable to meet the demands associated with an ageing workforce ([Falkenroth-Naidu et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b104)). In this regard, the next few years will prove especially trying as there will be an outflow of older employees and thus a loss of their collective knowledge and experience ([van de Kamp, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b328)). The challenge of an ageing workplace is further exacerbated by increased demand for labour as a result of increased production. A decade ago, companies operated at [lower production volumes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/low-volume-production "Learn more about lower production volumes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and thus required a smaller workforce. With a steady increase in demand since then, companies have ramped up production to capitalize on this trend, resulting in a pressing need for new staff. Digitalization offers potential solutions to these problems through the increases in labour productivity associated with technological development and the automation of tasks leading to the replacement of human labour for machine labour. The former follows from a trend that has been relatively consistent since the first industrial revolution, namely that technological advancement and adoption promote labour productivity ([OECD, 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b234)). In recent years, where digitalization has come to represent the forefront of technological innovation in industry, the pattern of productivity gains has continued ([Borowiecki et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b45), [Song et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b297)). As such, increased digitalization will result in a decreased need for labour to maintain production targets. The latter follows from a long history of machines replacing human labour. Indeed, this was the impetus of the Luddite [backlash](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/backlash "Learn more about backlash from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") against industrialization during the first industrial revolution. While, on the whole, digitalization will usher both increases and decreases in [labour demand](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/labor-demand "Learn more about labour demand from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), occupations that are physical and predictable such as machine operators will see significant replacement by machines in the coming decades ([Manyika et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b203)).

Another challenge facing the industry is that of reaching its [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") goals in the face of changing climate realities. Indeed, developing sustainable processes has been described as “the challenge of the 21st century for chemical engineering” ([Gwehenberger and Narodoslawsky, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772508124000231#b137)). As a result, the industry is facing increased pressure from regulators, investors, consumers, and activists to fundamentally change their operations to minimize its [environmental footprint](https

![The Chemical Engineer](/Content/img/header/logo.png)
![The Chemical Engineer](/Content/img/header/logo.png)

# Transforming the workforce with digital technologies

Article by Amanda Jasi

![](/media/19648/985986nidhug.jpg?&maxwidth=980&center=0.5,0.5&mode=crop&scale=both)

Automation execs share thoughts on how to help workers adapt

**OPTIMISING industrial transformation was the theme of the 2023 global** **Honeywell Users Group (HUG) conference, hosted in Florida, US, in June.**

Pramesh Maheshwari, president of automation major Honeywell Process Solutions, noted in his welcome session *(pictured, above)* that “all industries are transforming”. He acknowledged that the optimised route to transformation will vary across industries and companies, but highlighted key, universal trends including digital transformation and demographic change that he expects will influence the paths taken.

He said that as automation advances, demographic change will become more critical. He said 2.7m manufacturing workers are set to retire by 2030, which will cause a “massive” demographic shift.

“Knowledge and expertise accumulated over decades are departing, creating a pressure, a need to capture that wisdom. Simultaneously, a new generation with different approaches to working and learning is emerging.”

He said industry will face the twin challenge of preserving the expertise of experienced workers, while rapidly developing the skills of the new workers from generalist to specialist.

“We must support the changing workforce with tools and technologies to build competencies and ensure work is done safely and effectively,” he added.

## For improvement not replacement

While Maheshwari said that automation will replace workers in certain roles, he told *The Chemical Engineer* that he thinks this will be in high-risk and unsafe locations, “dirty” locations, and those which involve continuous repetitive tasks. Technology will augment people, allowing them to do a better job, he said.

In a breakout session with the press, he said: “It’s not about eliminating [people]. It’s about making them perform better, giving more options to them, so that when they’re doing the job, they have better gadgets with them, they have better skills with them, they have better knowledge with them…they are more competent.”

## Workforce360

Manas Dutta, general manager of workforce excellence at Honeywell, talked about technology which will help to address the workforce challenges and opportunities that Maheshwari discussed.

Honeywell’s Workforce360 is a programme that provides tools to improve human reliability, and data to enable safer and more efficient operations.

Dutta said that the process involves evaluation, training, prediction, and assistance.

Evaluation aims to understand the existing level of competency in a company and reveal gaps. Dutta said Honeywell’s Highly Augmented Lookahead Operations (HALO) Operator Advisor collects and analyses historical plant data to develop a benchmark on how best to handle different situations, and then it compares this to worker performance.

The workers are then trained using virtual reality (VR) that allows them to gain hands-on experience in different situations without the risks of making mistakes in a real plant. Dutta said through effective use of VR, learning that would normally take 2–3 years can be achieved in just 6–8 months. This allows companies to get a return on their training investment from a younger generation that only spends years in a role, compared to decades spent in roles by some of the retiring workforce. Dutta also noted that this method of learning helps to keep the attention of younger workers, who are habituated to technologies through the likes of gaming.

Using predictive insights offered by Honeywell technology, the trained staff can spot incoming problems and work to mitigate them in advance.

Lastly, assistance relates to field operators using devices in the field that allows them to get remote help from experts when needed, as well as to access historical and other data, so that they can make more informed decisions and solve problems faster.

He also noted benefits for companies including savings in on-the-job training, reduced plant downtime and overtime costs, and accident avoidance.

“All those things can be achieved by using technology as an enabler for the workforce to do their work right.”

Article by [Amanda Jasi](/authors/amanda-jasi/)

Staff reporter, The Chemical Engineer

enriched_points.expanded_content: Training programs focused on digital technologies, data science, and chemical engineering are essential to bridge skill gaps and enhance operational efficiency in chemical plants.
Fostering an innovative environment encourages employees to explore new digital solutions, leading to improved processes and increased competitiveness within the chemical industry.
Promoting teamwork between diverse departments enhances knowledge sharing, accelerates problem-solving, and drives the successful implementation of digital technologies in chemical operations.
